## reference · Visual Hierarchy in UX
* Visual hierarchy refers to the organization of design elements on a page to guide the eye to consume each element in the order of intended importance.
* A clear visual hierarchy guides the eye to the most important elements on the page.

### Color and Contrast
* Use color and contrast to create visual hierarchy, not the actual color of an element.
* Consider color saturation: bright colors for important items, less-saturated colors for less important items.
* Reserve warm bright colors (e.g., red) for warnings or errors.
* Don't use too many colors (limit to 2 primary and 2 secondary colors).
* Don't use too many contrast variations (no more than 3).
* Don't rely only on color to communicate visual hierarchy.

### Scale
* Use size to create visual hierarchy: bigger elements stand out more.
* Use no more than 3 sizes (small, medium, and large).
* Make the most important element the biggest.
* Limit the number of big elements to a maximum of 2.

### Grouping: Proximity and Common Regions
* Use grouping to direct attention to relevant areas of the screen.
* Convey grouping implicitly through proximity and white space or explicitly through enclosure (common region).
* Let elements breathe: more space around an element makes it more noticeable.
* Consider using a container (e.g., borders or backgrounds) to create visual hierarchy.

### Best Practices
* Define the hierarchy of content and key points before designing.
* Apply variations in color and contrast, scale, or grouping to create visual hierarchy.
* Test designs with target users to ensure the intended hierarchy is conveyed.

Sources: https://www.nngroup.com/articles/visual-hierarchy-ux-definition/
